intro.title: What is Java?
intro.description: Java is a class-based, object-oriented programming language designed to have minimal implementation dependencies. It’s widely used across enterprise, mobile application developement, and web platforms.
points: Platform independent via JVM.
Object-Oriented and secure.
Multithreading and garbage collection support.
Popular in enterprise, Android developement, and web development.

intro.title: JDK vs JRE vs JVM
points: JDK: The Java Development Kit (JDK) is a software development kit that provides a set of tools and libraries for developing Java applications. It includes the Java Runtime Environment (JRE), which is necessary for running Java programs, as well as development tools such as compilers and debuggers. The JDK is essential for developers who want to write, compile, and run Java code.

JRE:The Java Runtime Environment (JRE) is a package that provides the libraries and JVM required to run Java applications. It does not include development tools, making it suitable for users who only need to run Java programs without developing them. The JRE is a subset of the JDK and is included in the JDK installation.

JVM: The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) is a crucial component of the JRE that provides the runtime environment for Java programs. It is responsible for executing Java bytecode on any platform that supports JVM, making Java a “write once, run anywhere” language. The JVM is included in the JRE and is not installed separately.

intro.title: Features of Java
points: Simple : Easy to learn ,with syntax similar to c++ but without complex features.
Object-Oriented : Everything is treated as object
Platform-independent : Java code rund on any machine with a java virtual machile
Robust : Strong memory management, exception handling,and type checking
Multithreaded : Supports multiple programming
Architectural Neural : No dependency on processor architecture
Portable : Java bytecode can be transferred and run on any platform
High Performance : Just-In-Time(JIT) compiler improves performance.
Secure : Provides a secure environment with features like bytecode verification
sandboxing : Sandboxing is a security mechanism in Java that restricts the execution of untrusted code in a controlled environment
Distributed : Designed for nuilding distributed application
Dynamic : Supports dynamic linking of classes and methods .

intro.title: Disadvantages of Java
points: Slower Performance than Native Languages(like c or c++)
Memory consumption (Automatic garbage Collection and heavy JVM and not ideal for systems like low RAM)
Verbose Syntax (Java code can be very long-winded compared to modern languaes like python and kotlin .)
Poor GUI Support (Java's GUI liberaries (like Swing and AWT)(Outdated) (Incosstent in look across platforms) JavaFX is better ,but still not widely used for modern UIs.
No Control Over Low-Level Aspects (Java abstracts away memory management and hardware access)(not suitable for system level programming like device drivers or OS kernals)
Security Issues in Order Java Versions (Java Applets and Outdates plugins were once majore security risks through now deprecated,they left a legacy of trust issues .
Hight Startup Time (Java application take longer to start compared to native or scripting languages , this is due to jvm startup and class loading )
Backward Compatibility Bloat (Java is very backward compatible , But this leads to bloat and legacy code that must be supported in JVM)

install.title: Download and Install JDK
points: Go to the official Oracle website or OpenJDK provider.
Download the latest version of JDK suitable for your OS.
Run the installer and follow installation steps.
Verify installation using: java -version in terminal.

install.title: Setting Up Environment Variables
points: Open System Properties → Advanced → Environment Variables.
Create a new system variable named JAVA_HOME.
Set JAVA_HOME to your JDK installation path.
Add %JAVA_HOME%\bin to the Path variable.
Verify using: javac -version in terminal.

install.title: Writing and Running First Java Program
points: Create a file named HelloWorld.java.
Write a simple main method.
Compile using: javac HelloWorld.java.
Run using: java HelloWorld.

install.title: Using IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ IDEA, VS Code)
points: Download and install your preferred IDE.
Create a new Java project.
Write and run Java programs easily with built-in tools.
Use debugger and extensions for better development.

identifier.title: Definition of Identifiers
identifier.description: An identifier is the name used to identify classes, methods, variables, labels, and other user-defined elements in Java. In simple terms, identifiers are the names you give to elements in your program.

identifier.title: Rules for Naming Identifiers
identifier.description: Java has strict rules for naming identifiers. If you break any rule, the compiler will throw an error.
points: ✔️ Can contain letters (A-Z, a-z), digits (0-9), underscores (_), and dollar signs ($).
❌ Cannot start with a digit.
❌ Cannot use Java reserved keywords (e.g., int, class, public).
✔️ Can be of any length.
✔️ Java is case-sensitive (name and Name are different).
✔️ Unicode characters are allowed (avoid unless necessary).
❌ No spaces allowed in identifiers.

identifier.title: Valid Identifier Examples
Invalid Identifier Examples
identifier.code:
int 1stNumber; // Starts with a digit
String full name; // Contains space
float class; // 'class' is a reserved keyword

identifier.title: Java Naming Conventions (CamelCase, PascalCase)
identifier.description: Java developers follow standard naming conventions for readability and consistency.

table.description: First letter of each word capitalized

DataType.title: Definition of Data Types
points: Every data has some type, and that type is known as a Data Type.
Data type defines which type of data a variable can hold or store in memory.

DataType.title: Types of Data Types
points: 1. Primitive Data Type
2. Non-Primitive Data Type

DataType.title: Primitive Data Types
DataType.description: These are the data types that will store only values. To use these primitive data types, we have to follow certain rules.
points: If we want to use a long value in our program, we will use L/l at the end of the value as a suffix.
To differentiate in between float and double value, we will use F/f for float and D/d for double value as a suffix.
Using D/d is not mandatory; decimal values are double by default.
Char values use single quotes and contain only one character.
Boolean values must always be lowercase.

DataType.title: Non-Primitive Data Types
DataType.description: These are the data types that will store the objects only.Non-primitive data types (also called reference types) are types that refer to objects and do not store the actual data directly. They are created by the programmer (except for built-in ones like String, Array, etc.).Unlike primitive types (like int, char, etc.), non-primitive types store memory addresses (references) of objects.

DataType.title: Example Program

typecasting.title: TYPE CASTING
points: The process of converting one data type value into another data type is known as Typecasting.
It is mostly applied to primitive data types (except boolean).
This process is also called Primitive Data Type Casting.
There are 2 types of Typecasting in Java:
a. Implicit Type Casting
b. Explicit Type Casting

typecasting.title: IMPLICIT TYPE CASTING
points: Also known as: Widening or Upcasting
When a smaller data type is converted into a bigger data type automatically by the compiler.
There is no loss of data.
Happens in this order:
byte → short → int → long → float → double

typecasting.title: Explicit Type Casting
points: Also known as: Narrowing or Downcasting
When a bigger data type is converted into a smaller data type manually by the programmer.
There may be loss of data (especially fractional or overflow values).
This typecasting requires the use of type in brackets.

typecasting.title: Example Programs

variables.title: What is a Variable?
variables.description: Variable is a name or reference to a memory allocation where we are going to store our data.Data can be values as well as objects.
points: There are 2 parts of variable.
1. Declaration
2. Initialisation
Declaration is mandatory whereas initialization is optional.
If we are not initializing, JVM will provide default value according to the data type.

variables.title: Types of Variables
variables.description: categorization of the variable has been done on the basis of where we are declaring the variable and how we are declaring the variable.
points: 1. Non-Static variable
2. Static variable
3. Local Variable

variables.title: Non-Static Variable
variables.description: These are the variables whose values differs from object to object. Every object will have a separate copy of non-static variable.
points: The non-static variables are declared inside the class but outside the method block or constructor.
There is only one way in which we can access a non-static variable and that is by the help of object reference.
The non-static variables are stored in Heap area.
The memory of the non-static variable will be allocated after the creation of the object and that to be inside of the object.
Two non-static variable names cannot be same.

variables.title: Static Variable
variables.description: These variables have the same value for all objects of the class. Only one copy of a static variable is created and shared among all objects. Static variables are also called Class Variables.
points: Static variables are declared inside the class but with the static keyword, and outside of method blocks or constructors.
There are two ways to access a static variable:
1.	Using class name (Recommended)
2.	Using object reference
Static variables are stored in the Method Area (also called Class Area).
Memory for static variables is allocated only once when the class is loaded by the JVM.

variables.title: Local Variable
variables.description: A local variable is declared inside a method, constructor, or block. It is created when the method is called and destroyed when the method finishes. Local variables are not accessible outside the method.
points: Local variables must be initialized before use.
They are stored in the Stack area.
No default values are given by JVM to local variables.

operators.title: Operator Definition & Types
operators.description: Operators are the special symbols using which we can perform certain operations on operands.There are 3 types of operator present in java and these categorization has been done on the basis of how many operands we are using.
points: Unary Operator
Binary Operator
Ternary Operator

operators.title: Unary Operators
operators.description: Whenever we are performing any operation using only one operand, it is known as Unary Operator.
points: Increment Operator (++): It is a type of operator when used, will always increase the value of the variable by adding 1 to it. There are 2 types of increment operation.
1.Pre-Increment (++x) : In pre-increment, first the value will be increased then after it will get assigned.
2.Post-Increment (x++) : In post increment first the value will be assigned then after it will get incremented.
Decrement Operator (--) : It is a type of operator when used will always decrease the value of the variable by substracting 1 to it. There are 2 types of Decrement operation.
1.Pre-Decrement Operation(--x) : Whenever we are performing pre-decrement operation then it will first decrease the value then after it will assign.
2.Post-Decrement Operation(x--) : Whenever we are performing post-decrement operation then it will first assign the value thenafter it will get decremented.
Bitwise Complement (~) : In bitwise compliment it will first add 1 to the value then it will change the symbol of the final output.
Boolean Complement (!) : In Boolean compliment it will just change the Boolean value from true to false and false to true.

operators.title: Binary Operators
operators.description: Binary operators are the operators that will be used to perform operation with 2 operands.
points: Arithmetic Operators : These are the operators that will perform arithmetic operations like addition, subtraction,  etc.  They are:-	'+' :-add 	'-' :-subtract	 '*' :-multiply 	'/' :-divide '%' :-modulo

When we are using mod or divide operator both will perform division operation but the outputwill be different because mod will always take remainder as the output and divide will take quotient as the output.
String Concatenation : It is a process of merging two operand where both the operands can be string or any one of the the operand must be a string value.

-->In order to perform string concatenation we will be using add/plus (+) operator and it is also known as Overloaded operator because it can perform both addition and concatenation  simultaneously in the same line.

Assignment Operator : It is a type of operator which will assign value to the variable and variable to the object. For primitive it will be value to variable *(right to left) and for non-primitive it will be variable to object  *(left to right).
Relational Operators : These are the operators which will be used to check the relationship between 2 operands like '>' :-greater than '<' :-lesser than
Some compound relational operator are:-	'>=' :-greater than equal to '<=' :-lesser than equal to
The output of the Relational operator will always be in the form of Boolean expression.

Equality Operators : It is a type of operator which will be checking whether both the operand are equal or not. If it is same then the output will be true otherwise false. Equality operator's output will also be in the boolean expression. It is applicable for both primitive as well as non primitive data type.

Not equal Operators : It is type of operator which will be checking whether the operand are not equal or same. If not equal then it will give true as the output other wise false. Not equality operator's output will also be in the boolean expression. It is applicable for both primitive as well as non primitive data type.
Bitwise Operators : There are 3 types of BITWISE operator. 1.Bitwise AND (&) 2.Bitwise OR (|)  3.Bitwise XOR (^)
1.Bitwise AND (&) : In bitwise AND operator it will give us true as the output if both the operands output is true otherwise false.
2.Bitwise OR (|) : In bitwise OR operator it will give us true if any one of the operands output is true otherwise false.
3.Bitwise XOR (^) : In bitwise XOR operator it will give as true if both the operands output are different.
Logical Operators : In logical operator the execution of the 2nd operand is based on the output of the first operand. There are 2 types of Logical Operator:- 1.Logical AND (&&)  2.Logical OR (||)
1.Logical AND (&&) : In logical AND operator the 2nd operand will be executed if and only if(iff) the output of the first operand is true.
2.Logical OR (||) : In logic OR operator the 2nd operand will only be executed iff the output of the first operand is false.

operators.title: Ternary Operator
operators.description: Whenever we are using 3 operands to perform any operation then it is known as Ternary Operator.
points: Also known as Conditional Operator (?:)
Syntax: (condition)?Statement1:Statement2;
If the output of the condition is true then the statement 1 will be executed and when the output of the condition is false then statement 2 will be executed.

flowControl.title: 🌊 Java Flow Control
flowControl.description: Flow control refers to the order in which individual instructions, statements, or function calls are executed or evaluated in a Java program. By default, Java programs execute from top to bottom, but we can change this flow using Flow Control Statements.
points: ✅ Categories of Flow Control Statements
1. Selection Statements (Decision-Making)
2. Iteration Statements (Loops)
3. Transfer Statements (Jump/Branching)

flowControl.title: 1. Selection Statements (Decision Making)
flowControl.description: Used to choose between different paths of execution based on conditions.
points:
a. if-else Statement :-
      _________________________ 

      Syntax:
if (condition) {
    // Executes if condition is true
} else {
    // Executes if condition is false
}
      Rules:
      • Condition must be a boolean expression.
      • Only one block is executed.

      Special Case:,
      • If else is missing, and the condition is false, nothing will be executed.

      b. else-if Ladder :- 
      _____________________________
      Used when we need to check multiple conditions.

      Special Case:,
      • Once a condition evaluates to true, the rest are skipped.,
      • All conditions are checked in top-down order.,

      c. switch Statement :- 
      ____________________________
      Used to replace long else-if ladders for checking multiple fixed values.
      Supported data types (Java 7+):
      • byte, short, char, int, enum, String

      Special/Exceptional Cases:
      • break is optional; if omitted, execution falls through.
      • default is optional but recommended.
      • switch does not support ranges like case 1-3:.

flowControl.title: 2. Iteration Statements (Loops)
flowControl.description: Used to execute a block of code repeatedly.
points:
a. for Loop"
      ________________________________________
      Special Cases:
      • All three parts (init, condition, update) are optional.
      • Multiple variables can be initialized.
      
      b. while Loop
      ________________________________________
      Special Case:
      • If the condition is false initially, loop body won't execute at all.
     
      c. do-while Loop
       ________________________________________
      Special Case:
      • Guarantees at least one execution of loop body.

flowControl.title: 3. Transfer Statements (Jump/Branching)
flowControl.description: Used to change the normal flow of control in loops/switch.
points:
a. break
      ________________________________________
      Used to exit from switch or loop (for, while, do-while).

       Special Case : 
      • Breaks only from the nearest loop or switch.

      b. continue
      ________________________________________
      Skips current iteration and jumps to next cycle.

       Special Case :
      • Does not terminate the loop.

      c. return (Bonus)
      ________________________________________
       Used to exit from a method and return control to the caller.

classes.title: What is a Class?
classes.description: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. It defines properties (variables) and behaviours (methods) that its objects will have. Classes are fundamental to Java's Object-Oriented Programming paradigm.

classes.title: Syntax of a Class
Types of Classes
classes.description: Java provides different types of classes.
points: Concrete Class : A normal class that is fully implemented and can be instantiated.It contains constructors, fields, access modifiers, and can be extended by other classes.
Abstract Class :	A class that cannot be instantiated and serves as a blueprint for subclasses.
It contains both abstract methods (methods without implementation) and concrete methods (methods with implementation).
It Must be extended by subclasses to implement abstract methods.
It provides a common structure for related classes.
Final Class :A class declared using the final keyword and it cannot be extended by any other class.
It ensures class behaviour remains unchanged and protected from modifications
It can contain final methods and final variables
The Final Class cannot be inherited, Final Methods cannot be overridden in subclasses, and Final Variables values cannot be changed after initialization.

classes.title: Inner Classes
classes.description: A class within another class is called a nested class. It helps in better organization and encapsulation.There are four types of nested classes:
points: Static Inner Class : It is defined using the static keyword inside a class and access only static members of the outer class.It does not require an instance of the outer class to be created.
Non-Static Inner Class:A regular class inside another class.Requires an instance of the outer class to be created.
Local Inner Class : Defined inside a method.Accessible only within the method where it is defined.
Anonymous Inner Class : A class without a name.Used for implementing interfaces or extending classes inline.

methods.title: What is a Method?
methods.description: A method is a block of code that performs a specific task. It is used to define behaviour in a class and can be called multiple times to reuse the functionality, making programs more efficient and organized.

methods.title: Syntax of a Method
points: A method in Java consists of a declaration and implementation:
Declaration (Method Signature):- Includes access modifier, return type, method name, parameters.
Implementation (Method Body):- Contains the logic inside {} brackets.

methods.title: Types of Methods
points: Non-Static (Instance) Method: These methods belong to objects, meaning you need to create an object to call them.They can access instance variables and other non-static methods and static methods.
Static Method: These belong to the class itself. They can be called directly, with object name, or using the class name.

methods.title: Arguments in Methods
methods.description: Method argument is known as the input to the method.There are 2 types of method based on the argument.
points: With No Argument Method: No parameters in the method declaration
Parameterized Method: It accepts arguments to process data.
Primitive as Argument: Passes basic data types (byte, short, int, char, float, double, long, boolean ).
Non-Primitive as Argument: Passes objects as arguments.
Both Primitive & Non-Primitive: Takes a mix of both.

methods.title: Return Types in Methods
methods.description: Return type represents the output of a method.There are 3 types of return type.
points: Void (No Return Value): Performs operations but does not return anything.
Primitive Return Type: Returns basic values like int, double, boolean.
Non-Primitive Return Type: Returns objects, arrays, or collections.
How to print the return value of return statement:-
1. by calling that method inside the printing statement.
2. by storing it in a variable and then printing that variable.

methods.title: Important Notes
points: We can call a method inside a method but we cannot declare a method inside a method.
We can declare more then 1 method(user defined) inside a class.
We cannot create a object of a method.
We have to call a method in order to execute it ,it will not get executed or call automatically ,only main method is a method which will get execute automatically rest every method we are declaring.

inheritance.title: What is Inheritance?
inheritance.description: Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties and behaviours from another class. It promotes code reusability, reduces redundancy, and enhances maintainability.
points:
There are 5 type of Inheritance present in java and they are:-
1.Single Inheritance
2.Multi Level Inheritance
3.Multiple Inheritance
4.Hierarchical Inheritance
5.Hybrid Inheritance

inheritance.title: 1. Single Inheritance
inheritance.description: Single inheritance occurs when a child class (subclass) inherits from only one parent class (superclass).

inheritance.title: 2. Multi-Level Inheritance
inheritance.description: Multi-level inheritance occurs when a class inherits from another class, which in turn inherits from another class (forming a chain).

inheritance.title: 3. Multiple Inheritance (Not Supported with Classes)
inheritance.description: Multiple inheritance allows a class to inherit from more than one superclass. However, Java does NOT support multiple inheritance with classes to avoid ambiguity issues, Interfaces can be used to achieve multiple inheritance.

inheritance.title: 4. Hierarchical Inheritance
inheritance.description: Hierarchical inheritance occurs when multiple child classes inherit from the same parent class.

inheritance.title: 5. Hybrid Inheritance (Not supported directly in Java)
inheritance.description: Hybrid inheritance is a combination of two or more types of inheritance. Java does not support hybrid inheritance directly with classes when multiple Inheritance is there,but interfaces can be used to achieve it.

methodOverloading.title: Definition of Method Overloading
methodOverloading.description: This is a feature in Java that allows multiple methods with the same name but different parameters.•	It helps in improving code readability and maintainability.Increases flexibility by allowing different types or numbers of arguments.

methodOverloading.title: Rules for Method Overloading
points: Methods must have the same name.
Methods must have different parameter lists (number, type, or order of parameters).
Return type can be different.
Cannot overload methods solely based on return type.

methodOverloading.title: Ways to Achieve Method Overloading
points: 1. By Changing Number of Parameters:
Example: calculate(int a), calculate(int a, int b).
2. By Changing Type of Parameters:
Example: print(int num), print(String text).
3. By Changing Order of Parameters:
Example: display(int a, double b), display(double b, int a).

methodOverloading.title: Advantages of Method Overloading
points: Reduces redundant code by using a single method name.
Makes code more intuitive and readable.
Allows different ways of calling the same function with different inputs.

methodOverloading.title: Example of Method Overloading

methodOverriding.title: Definition of Method Overriding
methodOverriding.description: Occurs when a subclass redefines a method from its parent class. Ensures dynamic method dispatch during runtime. Provides polymorphism, allowing flexibility in program behaviour.

methodOverriding.title: Rules for Method Overriding
points: The overriding method must have the same name and same parameter list as the parent method.
The method in the parent class must be inherited by the subclass.
The overriding method cannot have a more restrictive access modifier than the original.
The overriding method cannot override static, final, or private methods.

methodOverriding.title: Co-Variant Concept
methodOverriding.description: If we want to change the return type in method overriding then we will go for co-variant concept . In order to perform co-variant concept we have to follow some rules:-
points: Rule 1: We can only use non-primitive data type.
Rule 2: Parent class return type must be the parent of child return type.

methodOverriding.title: Usage of super Keyword in overriding
methodOverriding.description: Used to call the parent class's method.
points: Helps retain original behaviour while extending functionality.
Ensures access to superclass implementations when needed.

methodOverriding.title: Dynamic Method Dispatch (Runtime Polymorphism)
points: Overriding enables method calls to be determined at runtime.
Helps in achieving loose coupling and extensibility.

methodOverriding.title: Example of Method Overriding
Annotations in Method Overriding
points: @Override annotation is used.
Indicates that a method is overriding a superclass method
Helps detect errors if the method does not correctly override

cards.title: Access Modifiers
cards.description: Control the accessibility or scope of classes, methods and variables.
points: Public: Accessible from anywhere.
Private: Accessible only within declared class.
Protected: Accessible within same package and subclasses.
Default(No Modifier): Accessible within same package.
public > protected > default > private
while coming from parent to child class, the scope of access modifier should increase but should not decrease
parent class method modifier --> public    protected             default
child class method modifier  --> public    public/protected      public/protected/default
Note:- Note: private modifier cant be overridden

cards.title: Non-Access Modifiers (Provide additional functionality)
cards.description: Final: Prevents modification (e.g., final class cannot be inherited, final method cannot be overridden
points: Final Class: if a class is declared as final then we cant create child class of that class or inheritance is not possible
Final Method: if a method is declared as final, we cant override that method because its implementations get fixed
Final Variable: if a variable is declared as final we cant change the value of that variable
Note: every method present inside final class is by default final but variables need not be final

cards.title: Abstract Modifier
cards.description: Used with abstract classes and abstract methods.
points: Abstract Class: Abstract class is a class whose object can't be created. Abstract class can contain abstract method as well as concrete method (normal method)
Abstract method: if a method is abstract then class must be abstract, but if a class is abstract then method need not be abstract
Abstract Variable: Abstract modifier is not applicable for variable. If a method is abstract then class must be abstract, but if a class is abstract then method need not be abstract

cards.title: Other Non-access Modifiers
points: Static: Belongs to the class rather than an instance.
Transient: Skips serialization of variables.
Synchronized: Ensures thread safety for methods.
Volatile: Ensures variable updates are visible across threads.

cards.title: What is a Constructor?
cards.description: A constructor is a special method used to initialize objects in Java . It is automatically called when an object is created . Constructors share the same name as the class and do not have a return type.

cards.title: Rules for Naming Constructors
points: 1. A constructor must have the same name as the class in which it resides.
2. Unlike methods, constructors do not have a return type, not even void.
3. Cannot Be Abstract, Final, Static, or Synchronized – These modifiers are not allowed in constructor declarations.
4. You can use access modifiers (like public, private, or protected) to control which other classes can call the constructor.

cards.title: Types of Constructors
points: a) Default Constructor :-
✔ No parameters
✔ Automatically created if no constructor is defined in the class
✔ Used for basic object initialization
b) Parameterized Constructor :-
✔ Takes arguments to set values at object creation
✔ Allows flexible initialization of object properties
c) Copy Constructor :-
✔ Creates a new object by copying values from an existing object
✔ Used for cloning objects

cards.title: Constructor Overloading
points: ✔ Multiple constructors in the same class with different parameters
✔ Allows flexibility in object creation

cards.title: Usage of this Keyword in Constructors
points: ✔ Refers to current class instance
✔ Used to differentiate between instance variables and parameters
✔ Can call another constructor from the same class using this()

cards.title: Constructor Chaining
points: ✔ Calls one constructor from another in the same class
✔ Helps avoid redundant code

cards.title: Advantages of Constructors
points: ✔ Automatic execution when object is created.
✔ Encapsulation ensures safe object initialization
✔ Reduces redundant code using constructor chaining

cards.title: What is a Factory Method?
cards.description: A Factory Method is a static method that is called using the class name and returns the instance of that class.

cards.title: Example of Factory Method
points: ✔ The Factory Method creates objects without exposing the creation logic.
✔ Useful for encapsulating object creation and improving flexibility.

cards.title: What is a Singleton Class?
cards.description: A Singleton class is a class that allows only one object to be created, and that object is created and managed inside the class itself.

cards.title: How to Create Our Own Singleton Class?
cards.description: We can create our own Singleton class by using a factory method and by declaring the constructor as private. The factory method ensures that only one object is created, and the private constructor restricts object creation from outside the class.

cards.title: Example of Singleton Class
points: ✔ Step 1: Declare a private static variable for the single instance.
✔ Step 2: Make the constructor private to prevent external instantiation.
✔ Step 3: Create a public static method to return the single instance.

cards.definition: It is the process of binding or wrapping up of data members along with its data handler methods i.e., getters & setters
advantages: We can protect the data from unauthorized access.
We can make the data readonly or writeonly.
specifications: The bean class must be a public non-abstract class.
We can make the data readonly or writeonly.
example: public void setAge(int age) { this.age = age; }
public void setPerc(double perc) { this.perc = perc; }

sections.title: Object Class Overview
sections.description: In Java, Object is the superclass of all classes. It is part of java.lang package and provides a foundation for every class. Every class in Java implicitly inherits from Object if no other superclass is specified.
keyPoints: Object class is the parent of all classes in Java.
Part of java.lang package - automatically imported.
Provides basic methods like toString(), equals(), hashCode(), etc.
These methods can be overridden to provide custom behavior.
Forms the root of the Java class hierarchy.

sections.title: Common Methods of Object Class
sections.description: The Object class provides several fundamental methods that are inherited by all Java classes. These methods can be overridden to provide class-specific behavior.

details: It is required to return content of the object and not address of the object.(String, Wrapper, and All collections implemented classes etc.)
Returns a string representation of the object.
Default implementation returns className@hashCode.
Should be overridden to provide meaningful string representation.
It is a method which will perform comparison between two objects for equality.
This method will compare both address as well as content of an object.
It will compare content when it has been overridden in the class where objects are being compared.
Default implementation uses == operator (reference comparison).
Should be overridden to compare object contents rather than references.
Must satisfy reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and consistent properties.
If equals() is overridden, hashCode() should also be overridden.

sections.title: Complete Example
sections.description: Here's a comprehensive example showing how to properly override Object class methods in a custom class.

sections.title: String Class Overview
sections.description: The String class in Java is a predefined class in the java.lang package that represents a sequence of characters. It is immutable, meaning once a String object is created, its content cannot be changed.
keyPoints: Immutable: Cannot be changed once created.
Stored in the String Pool to save memory.
Rich set of built-in methods like length(), substring(), etc.
Overloaded '+' operator for concatenation.
Implements Serializable, Comparable, and CharSequence interfaces.

sections.title: String Object Creation Methods
sections.description: There are multiple ways to create a String object in Java. Each method has different memory behaviors and use cases.

details: String literals are stored in a special memory area called the String Constant Pool (SCP).
When a string literal is created, JVM first checks if that literal already exists in the SCP.
If it exists, the existing reference is reused (no new object is created).
If it does not exist, a new String object is created in the SCP, and its reference is returned.
This mechanism improves memory efficiency by avoiding duplicate String objects.
String literals are implicitly final and can be shared across multiple parts of the application.
Comparison using `==` works for string literals because they refer to the same object in the SCP.
This approach always creates a new String object in the heap memory, regardless of SCP contents.
Even if the same value exists in the String Constant Pool (SCP), a new object is created in the heap.
The string literal ("abc") is still stored in SCP if not already present — used as a constructor argument.
So effectively, two objects may be created: one in SCP and one in the heap.
This method breaks reference equality (`==`) even if the content is the same.
Used rarely unless object uniqueness is necessary (e.g., testing memory behavior).
Comparison using `.equals()` returns `true` because it compares values, but `==` returns `false`.

sections.title: Overview of StringBuffer
sections.description: StringBuffer is a predefined class in Java (inside java.lang package) introduced to overcome the limitations of the String class.
keyPoints: It is used to create mutable string objects.
It was introduced to resolve issues related to immutability in the String class.
String class creates a new object even for small changes, leading to poor memory management.
StringBuffer allows modifications without creating a new object, improving performance.

sections.title: Why StringBuffer was Introduced
sections.description: StringBuffer helps solve performance and memory issues that arise when using immutable String objects.
keyPoints: While performing operations on String objects, JVM creates a new object for every small change.
This leads to poor memory usage and performance overhead.
To fix this, StringBuffer allows changes within the same object (mutable).
Useful in scenarios where content changes frequently (e.g., loops, real-time logs, editors).

sections.title: Example of StringBuffer
sections.description: Here's a basic example showing how StringBuffer works in Java:

sections.title: Difference Between String and StringBuffer
sections.description: Here is a comparison between String and StringBuffer based on various factors:

rows: When we concatenate too many strings, it takes longer and uses more memory because it creates a new instance each time.
When concatenating two strings, StringBuffer is faster and uses less memory.
When executing a concatenation operation, the String class is slower.
When performing concatenation operations, the StringBuffer class is faster.
The equals() method of the Object class is overridden by the String class. As a result, the equals() method can be used to compare the contents of two strings.
The equals() function of the Object class is not overridden by the StringBuffer class.
It overrides the object class's equal() and hashcode() methods.
It cannot override the object class's equal() and hashcode() methods.
String constant pool is used by the String class.
Heap memory is used by StringBuffer.

constructors.description: Creates an empty string buffer with default capacity of 16.
Creates a string buffer with the specified capacity.
Creates a string buffer with 16 + length of the provided string.

methods.name: delete(int begin, int end)
methods.description: Deletes characters from begin to end.

methods.name: trimToSize()
methods.description: Trims the capacity to the current size.

stringBuilderComparison.title: StringBuilder Class
stringBuilderComparison.definition: A pre-defined class in the java.lang package. It is exactly the same as the StringBuffer class, except for the following differences:

comparison.stringBuilder: None of the methods are synchronized

comparison.stringBuffer: Slower due to synchronization (more time and complexity)
comparison.stringBuilder: Faster as no synchronization is used (better performance)

sections.title: Wrapper Class Overview
sections.description: A Wrapper class in Java is a type of class that provides a mechanism to convert primitive data types into objects and vice versa. Java is not a purely object-oriented programming language, because it works with primitive data types. These eight primitive data types (int, short, byte, long, float, double, char, and boolean) are not objects.
keyPoints: Java uses Wrapper Classes to wrap primitive data types into objects.
Wrapper classes make Java code fully object-oriented by allowing primitives to be used as objects.
They enable primitives to be added to collections like ArrayList, HashSet, and HashMap.
Wrapper classes provide utility methods for conversion between primitive types and strings, as well as for manipulating binary, octal, and hexadecimal representations.
Unlike primitive types, Wrapper classes can be null, making them useful in cases where a null value is required for a primitive type.

sections.title: Need for Wrapper Classes in Java
sections.description: Wrapper classes are essential in Java to make primitive types act like objects. They also offer several utility methods to manipulate data effectively.
keyPoints: Wrapper classes allow primitive types to participate in object-oriented behavior.
They make it possible to store primitive types in collections, where only objects can be stored.
Wrapper classes provide methods for converting primitive types to String objects and vice-versa.
They enable conversion to various bases like binary, octal, or hexadecimal.
Null values can be assigned to wrapper classes, unlike primitive types, which cannot be null.

sections.title: Wrapper Class Hierarchy
sections.description: The hierarchy of wrapper classes in Java shows how the different wrapper classes for primitive types inherit from the `Object` class and how the `Number` class is the parent of numerical wrappers.
keyPoints: The hierarchy starts from `Object`, the root class of all classes in Java.
The `Number` class is a subclass of `Object` and serves as the parent for numerical wrapper classes.
Boolean and Character are independent and do not inherit from `Number`.
The numerical classes include `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, and `Double`.

Integer.note: The Integer class provides two constructors: one that accepts a primitive int and another that accepts a String as an argument.

Double.note: The Double class provides two constructors: one that accepts a primitive double and another that accepts a String as an argument.

Boolean.note: The Boolean class accepts primitive true/false or String arguments ('true' case-insensitive).

sections.title: Overview
sections.description: Utility methods in Java's wrapper classes provide convenient ways to convert between primitives, strings, and wrapper objects. These static methods are alternatives to constructors and help with type conversion.
keyPoints: Common utility methods include valueOf(), xxxValue(), parseXxx(), and toString().
They are usually static and belong to wrapper classes like Integer, Double, etc.
They avoid unnecessary object creation and improve performance.

sections.title: valueOf() Method
sections.description: The valueOf() method creates a wrapper object from a primitive or a String representation. It is preferred over constructors.
keyPoints: Creates a wrapper object from primitive or string.
Preferred over 'new' keyword due to internal caching.

sections.title: xxxValue() Method
sections.description: The xxxValue() methods convert a wrapper object to its corresponding primitive type. These methods are instance methods of wrapper classes like Integer, Float, etc.
keyPoints: Available in all numeric wrapper classes.
For byte and short, if the value exceeds the range, it wraps around (e.g., 130 as byte → -126).
Character and Boolean have one method each: charValue() and booleanValue().

sections.title: parseXxx() Method
sections.description: The parseXxx() methods are used to convert a String to a corresponding primitive value. These are static methods of the wrapper classes.
keyPoints: Present in all wrapper classes except Character.

keyPoints.code:
int x = Integer.parseInt("10");
double d = Double.parseDouble("3.14");

keyPoints: Useful for converting numeric Strings into primitive data for calculations.

sections.title: toString() Method
sections.description: The toString() method converts wrapper objects or primitives into their String representation. Each wrapper class overrides toString().
keyPoints: Returns a String representation of the object or value.
You can convert primitives or wrapper objects to strings easily.

sections.title: Overview
sections.description: Before Java 1.4v,we can't provide wrapper object in place of primitive and primitive in the place of wrapper object,All the required conversion should be performed explicitly by the programmer using methods.From Java 1.5v onwards, the compiler handles this automatically.
keyPoints: Autoboxing: Converting primitive → wrapper object.
Unboxing: Converting wrapper object → primitive.
Compiler performs these conversions automatically during assignment, method invocation, etc.

keyPoints.code:
int x = new Integer(10); // unboxing
Integer y = 20;           // autoboxing

sections.title: Autoboxing
sections.description: Autoboxing is the automatic conversion of a primitive datatype into its corresponding wrapper object.
keyPoints: Handled by the compiler using valueOf() method internally.

sections.title: Unboxing
sections.description: Unboxing is the automatic conversion of a wrapper object to its corresponding primitive datatype.
keyPoints: Compiler uses xxxValue() methods internally (e.g., intValue(), doubleValue()).

keyPoints.code:
Integer i = new Integer(20);
int x = i; // Internally: i.intValue();

sections.title: What is Collection Framework?
sections.description: The Java Collection Framework is the combination of several predefined interfaces, their child interfaces and their implemented classes.
keyPoints: Supports both legacy and modern classes (like Vector, ArrayList).
Enables developers to work with different data structures in a consistent way.
Offers interfaces like List, Set, Queue, and Map along with their implementations.
Enhances code reusability and readability by providing a set of generic interfaces and classes for various data structures.

sections.title: Why was Collection Framework introduced?
sections.description: Before Java 1.2, arrays were the primary way to store groups of objects, but they had fixed sizes, lacked flexibility, and didn't support built-in methods for common operations. The Collection Framework overcame these limitations by offering dynamic and consistent data structures.
keyPoints: To eliminate the limitations of arrays (fixed size, type restriction).
To unify legacy classes (like Vector, Stack, Hashtable) under common interfaces.
To reduce boilerplate code when performing common data operations (add, remove, sort, etc.).
To provide algorithms like sorting and searching built into utility classes (Collections, Arrays).
To offer generic and type-safe collections using Java Generics (since Java 5).

sections.title: Difference between Array and Collection
sections.description: Arrays and Collections serve similar purposes but have fundamental differences in terms of size, type safety, functionality, and performance characteristics.

data.array: Fixed size (defined at creation time)
data.collection: Dynamic size (can grow/shrink at runtime)

data.array: Can store both primitives and objects
data.collection: Can only store objects (primitives are auto-boxed)

data.array: Homogeneous (same type elements)
data.collection: Can be heterogeneous, but generics provide type safety

data.array: Stored in contiguous memory locations
data.collection: Uses underlying data structures (linked lists, trees, etc.)

data.array: Faster access O(1) for index-based operations
data.collection: Varies by implementation (ArrayList O(1), LinkedList O(n))

data.array: Limited built-in methods (length property)
data.collection: Rich API (add, remove, contains, size, etc.)

data.array: Direct index access: arr[0]
data.collection: Method-based access: list.get(0)

data.array: Can store null values
data.collection: Can store null values (except some implementations)

sections.title: Collection Framework Hierarchy
sections.description: The Collection Framework is structured into a hierarchy of interfaces and classes, allowing consistent architecture for various data structures. Below is a visual representation of its hierarchy.

sections.title: What is Collection Interface?
sections.description: Whenever we want to represent a group of individual object as a single entity, then we will go for collection interface.
keyPoints: Part of `java.util` package and the root of the Collection hierarchy.
Extended by interfaces like List, Set, and Queue.
Provides core methods like `add()`, `remove()`, `contains()`, `isEmpty()`, and `size()`.
Cannot be instantiated directly, but its subinterfaces can be implemented.
Represents a group of objects known as elements.

sections.title: Collection Interface Methods
sections.description: The Collection interface provides several essential methods for manipulating collections. These methods form the foundation for all collection operations and are inherited by all collection types.

data.array: Adds the specified element to the collection
data.collection: list.add("Java"); // Returns true if added successfully

data.array: Adds all elements from specified collection
data.collection: list1.addAll(list2); // Adds all elements of list2 to list1

data.array: Removes the specified element from collection
data.collection: list.remove("Java"); // Returns true if element was removed

data.array: Removes all elements that are contained in specified collection
data.collection: list1.removeAll(list2); // Removes common elements

data.array: Retains only elements that are contained in specified collection
data.collection: list1.retainAll(list2); // Keeps only common elements

data.array: Removes all elements from the collection
data.collection: list.clear(); // Makes collection empty

data.array: Returns true if collection contains specified element
data.collection: boolean found = list.contains("Java");

data.array: Returns true if collection contains all elements of specified collection
data.collection: boolean hasAll = list1.containsAll(list2);

data.array: Returns true if collection contains no elements
data.collection: if(list.isEmpty()) { System.out.println("Empty"); }

data.array: Returns the number of elements in collection
data.collection: int count = list.size(); // Gets element count

data.array: Returns an iterator over elements in collection
data.collection: Iterator it = list.iterator(); // For traversing

data.array: Converts collection to array
data.collection: Object[] arr = list.toArray(); // Collection to array

Note: This category is used to store group of individual elements where the elements can be duplicated .
List is an interface whose object can not be created directly .
To Work with this category we have to use following implementation clas of list interface .
ListNote: -> List is the child interface of collection .
-> If we want to represent a group of individual where duplicates are allowed and insertion order is preserved. Then we should go for list .
-> We can differentiate duplicate Objects and we can maintain insertion order by means of index hence (index plays a crucial role in List .)
ArrayListNotes: -> ArrayList is an implementation class of Collection interface .
-> The underlying structure is resizable(internally it will use Array to store data)
ArrayListConstructor: ArrayList creates an empty ArrayList Obejct with default initila capacity 10 .
ArrayList reaches its maximum capacity then a new ArrayList Object will be created with (NewCapacity = (current Capacity * 3/2)+1
2>> ArrayList al = new ArrayList(int initial capacity);
Creates an empty Arraylist Object with the specified initial capacity .
Creates  an equivalent ArryaList Object for the given collection that is this constructort menat for inter conversation between Collection Objects .
important: -> ArrayList and vector classes implements Random Access interface so that any random element we can access with the same speed .
-> RandomAccess interface present in utill package and does not contain any methods . It is a marker interface .

Note: Linkedlist is one of the implementation classes of Collection interface .
The underlying data structure is double Linkedlist .
If our frequent operation is insertion or deletion in the middle then Linkedlist is thge best choice .
If our frequent operation is retrival then LinkedList is not best option .
Implementation Serilizable and Cloneable interface but  not RandomAccess .
LinkedListNotes: Note: Usually we can linkedloist to implement Stacks and Queues to provide support for this requirement Linkedlist class defines the following 6 specific methods .
LinkedlistConstructors: It creates an empty LinkedList Object .
To create an equivalent Linkedlist Object for the given Collection .
important: -> ArrayList is slower in insertion and deletion of elements because it internally requires shifting operations , But faster in accessing the elements because ArrayList use index position for every element .

-> LinkedList is faster in insertion and deletion of elements because it just require modifying the links of nodes instead of shifting operations,But slower in accessing the elements because Linkedlist does not use any index position .

Note: Vector is the implemntation class of List interface which is also used to store group individual objects where duplicate values are allowed .
Vector is exactly similar to ArrayList but Arraylist is not a synchronized class  where vector is a synchronized class .
Vector is also  called legacy class because it is available from Java 1.0 version .
Stack is a child of Vector and implements List interface .
Stack stores group of objects b using a mechanism called LIFO .
LIFO stands for Last in first out, it means last inserted element deleted first .
Methods: -> We can use all collection Methods .
-> We can also use legacy methods of Vector class like addElement() , removeElement() , setElementAt()...
-> But if we want to follow the LIFO mechanism , we should use Stack methods like follows .
E push(E obj) -: this method will add new element available on stack .
E pop() -: this method deletes the top element available on Stack .
E peek() -: this method just returns the top element available on stack .
